note: Per-card-type warm-throughput baselines for the perf-regression gate (scripts/perf_regression.py). The file nests one models block per card type under a 'cards' key (p150a, p300c, ...); the gate detects the card it is running on at runtime (tt-smi / kernel sysfs, mirroring tt_bio/main.py::_detect_p300_devices) and compares only against the matching card's baseline. Under each card-type key an optional 'machines' map keys baselines by physical machine id (socket.gethostname(), the repo's existing convention in tt_bio/runtime.py); the gate resolves a model's baseline from cards.<card_type>.machines.<machine_id>.models first and falls back to cards.<card_type>.models if no machine-specific entry exists for the detected machine. This guards within-card-type machine variance — e.g. qb1's p150a cards read ~30-36% slower than pc's p150a on the same models, so a baseline seeded on pc reads as a false regression on qb1 (and vice versa); a machine-specific entry overrides the card-level fallback per model, so a card type does not need a full per-machine block, only the models that actually differ per machine. A P300c baseline must never be judged against a P150a run — the P150 is a smaller chip and would read as a false 20-34% regression that is just the card, not the code. If a detected card type has no recorded baseline yet the gate FAILS loudly (every model NO BASELINE) rather than silently skipping. Protocol: scripts/perf_regression.py --model <m>; fold models fold examples/trpcage.yaml (20 aa, single-sequence) at 1 recycling cycle / 10 sampling steps / 1 diffusion sample; esmc-600m embeds 8x ubiquitin (76 aa) at batch 8; esmc-6b embeds 8x ubiquitin (76 aa) one-sequence-at-a-time (batch 1 nominal) -- the 6B sharded-TransformerEngine backbone (~13 GB resident) ignores batch_size and runs sequentially, so its seq/s is per-sequence throughput on a different runtime shape than 300m/600m, gated separately; boltzgen runs `tt-bio gen run examples/binder.yaml` (protein-anything, 4 designs) end-to-end and reports designs/s = num_designs / wall-clock (a single pipeline invocation, not a warm loop — the first design's first-kernel compile is included, so this is a conservative cold-inflated warm-throughput proxy; the cold fraction is stable across releases). Each fold/embed model: load once in-process, 2 warmup folds (absorb first-kernel compile, excluded), 5 timed folds, report median throughput. Model load + first-compile are excluded — this is a warm dispatch/throughput number, not a cold-start or production fold. It catches kernel/dispatch regressions; accuracy is scripts/release_gate.py's job. Update a baseline only via --update-baseline --note "<why this perf change is intended>" on a card of that type; never silently. Seed a new card type by running the gate on that card with --update-baseline; --update-baseline writes to the detected machine's machine-specific block so every baseline is tagged with the machine that produced it.

boltz2-affinity.note: seed tt-quietbox p150a boltz2-affinity perf baseline: the leg was only baselined on machine pc, so the full perf gate FAILed loudly with NO BASELINE on tt-quietbox (a gate-config gap, not a regression -- all 10 real perf models PASS within +/-15%). Seed the machine-specific entry from this warm run so the full all-models perf gate goes green on qb1.

tt-quietbox.note: seed tt-quietbox p150a boltz2-affinity perf baseline: the leg was only baselined on machine pc, so the full perf gate FAILed loudly with NO BASELINE on tt-quietbox (a gate-config gap, not a regression -- all 10 real perf models PASS within +/-15%). Seed the machine-specific entry from this warm run so the full all-models perf gate goes green on qb1.
